docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
A Liberty (.lib) file is a standard cell library description used by synthesis, timing analysis, and physical design tools. It contains timing, power, area, and functional information for each cell in a technology library.

Liberty files are developed by the foundry or library vendor and are one of the primary inputs to digital implementation flows.

docs.title: Why Liberty Files Are Important
docs.text:
Liberty files provide:

  • Cell functionality
  • Timing information
  • Power information
  • Area information
  • Pin characteristics
  • Operating conditions
  • Constraint definitions

Tools such as Fusion Compiler, PrimeTime, Design Compiler, OpenROAD, and Yosys use Liberty files during implementation and signoff.

docs.title: Liberty File in ASIC Flow
docs.text:
RTL
 ↓
Synthesis
 ↓
Gate-Level Netlist
 ↓
Place & Route
 ↓
Static Timing Analysis

      ↑
   Liberty
    (.lib)

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
A Liberty file typically contains:

library
├── Operating Conditions
├── Wire Load Models
├── Cell Definitions
│   ├── Pins
│   ├── Timing Arcs
│   ├── Power Data
│   └── Area
└── Constraints

docs.title: Technology Information
docs.text:
• Time Unit
  • Voltage Unit
  • Current Unit
  • Capacitance Unit
  • Resistance Unit

docs.title: Operating Conditions
docs.text:
Example:

operating_conditions(TT_0P80V_25C) {
    process : 1.0;
    voltage : 0.80;
    temperature : 25;
}

Parameters:

  • Process
  • Voltage
  • Temperature

docs.title: Cell Definition
docs.text:
Example:

cell(AND2X1) {
    area : 1.25;

    pin(A) {
        direction : input;
    }

    pin(B) {
        direction : input;
    }

    pin(Y) {
        direction : output;
    }
}

docs.title: Timing Arcs
docs.text:
Timing arcs define delay relationships between input and output pins.

Example:

timing() {
    related_pin : "A";
}

Common timing arc types:

  • Combinational
  • Rising Edge
  • Falling Edge
  • Setup
  • Hold
  • Recovery
  • Removal

docs.title: Delay Modeling
docs.text:
Delay depends on:

  • Input Transition
  • Output Load Capacitance
  • Process Corner
  • Voltage
  • Temperature

Liberty files typically use lookup tables to model delays accurately.

docs.title: Power Modeling
docs.text:
Power data includes:

  • Internal Power
  • Switching Power
  • Leakage Power

Example:

cell_leakage_power : 0.003;

docs.title: Common Standard Cells
docs.text:
• Buffer
  • Inverter
  • NAND
  • NOR
  • AOI
  • OAI
  • Multiplexer
  • Flip-Flop
  • Latch
  • Clock Gating Cell

docs.title: Library Corners
docs.text: Corner Process Voltage Temperature TT Typical Nominal 25°C SS Slow Low High FF Fast High Low

Library

library(my_library) {
}

cell(INVX1) {
}

docs.text:

}

docs.title: Timing
docs.text:

}

docs.title: Internal Power
docs.text:
internal_power() {
}

docs.title: Usage in Design Flow
docs.text: Stage Usage Synthesis Cell Mapping Placement Timing Estimation CTS Clock Optimization Routing Timing Updates STA Signoff Analysis Power Analysis Dynamic and Leakage Power

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The Liberty file is one of the most important inputs in ASIC implementation. It describes the functionality, timing, power, area, and constraints of standard cells and enables accurate synthesis, physical design, timing analysis, and power analysis throughout the design flow.
